abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as highly effective tools for modeling graph-structured data within recommender systems. Despite their success, real-world recommendation environments are often characterized by complex, heterogeneous relationships—such as social influences among users and knowledge-driven dependencies between items—that offer rich semantic cues for refining user preference learning. In this paper, we address the challenges of heterogeneous graph-enhanced relational learning. While contrastive self-supervised learning has recently gained traction in the field, existing methods often fail to fully capture the nuances of multi-relational data. To bridge this gap, we introduce Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning (HGCL), a framework designed to integrate diverse relational semantics into user-item interaction modeling via contrastive-enhanced knowledge transfer across multiple graph views. Recognizing that the impact of side information is inherently subjective, we further augment HGCL with meta-networks. This architectural choice enables a personalized knowledge transformer that facilitates adaptive contrastive augmentation tailored to individual users and items. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that HGCL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art recommendation paradigms. Detailed ablation studies further confirm the efficacy of our key components in driving performance gains. Our implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/HKUDS/HGCL.
